verdict: 2026-06-28 10:41:21 PDT — I would not quite build it this way yet. The broad direction fits the repo: a Markdown-only skill, frontmatter-only metadata, no new runtime dependency, the native-workflow stand-down, declarative selection/rubric intent, drift-only linting, and installer/docs wiring all match local doctrine. The remaining shape issues are in the central invariants the skill will teach future audits to copy: tool execution is not declarative enough to guarantee read-only runs, the new AUDIT taxonomy is not carried through the existing close lifecycle, and the maturity/risk rubric is still partly prose rather than one classification table.

findings.title: Tool table does not encode safe read-only invocations
findings.claim: Table A maps ecosystems to tool names, then step 4 says to `command -v <tool>` and run it read-only. That leaves the actual invocation to per-run judgment, even for formatter-shaped tools like prettier, black, and rustfmt whose naive commands can mutate the target repo. In a Markdown skill, the declarative table is the only durable place to centralize the non-destructive invariant.
findings.alternative: Make Table A a command-template table: detection marker, probe binary, read-only command/check mode, rationale text, and recommended-if-absent text. Use check/diff/no-write modes explicitly, and keep the architecture-review lens as a non-command entry rather than something step 4 treats like a binary.
findings.win: Prevents accidental target-repo writes, removes ad hoc per-ecosystem command selection, and gives the drift linter stable anchors for the core read-only contract.

findings.title: AUDIT backlog items are introduced without updating the close lifecycle
findings.claim: BACKLOG.md and ARCHITECTURE.md now say `AUDIT-` items flow through `/frame → /review → /close` like any other backlog line, but `/close` still only moves tracked `BUG-`/`OPS-` items to Done during release bookkeeping. That splits the public backlog taxonomy from the skill that consumes it, so audit findings can graduate into the loop but be stranded at close.
findings.alternative: Either make `/close` generic over any tracked backlog item, or explicitly include `AUDIT-` alongside `BUG-`/`OPS-` in its release-record step. Prefer the generic wording so future item kinds do not require another lifecycle edit.
findings.win: One lifecycle rule covers all backlog inflows, audit findings actually complete the promised path, and the taxonomy stops being duplicated across docs and skill procedure.

findings.title: Maturity and risk are still split between table and prose
findings.claim: Table B maps individual safeguards to absent flags, but maturity tier and risk level are derived in prose bullets below it, with extra high-risk triggers such as audit hits and security-sensitive domains outside the table. That partially reintroduces the ad hoc classification the design decision was meant to eliminate.
findings.alternative: Replace Table B with a compact condition matrix: priority/condition, maturity tier, risk level, required flags, and rationale. Put secret hits, vulnerable dependencies, CI/tests/pinning state, and security-sensitive-domain conditions in that same table.
findings.win: Centralizes the classification invariant, removes duplicate prose rules, and makes drift checks protect the actual tier/risk mapping rather than just the table heading.
